title: Mean, median, mode
deck: Three measures of center on the same data set. The mean is the arithmetic average; the median is the middle value of the sorted data; the mode is the most frequent value. Each answers a slightly different version of the same question.
objectives: Compute the mean, median, and mode of a data set; identify the appropriate measure of center for a given application.

content.text: The simplest summary of a data set is a single number that captures “where its center sits” on the number line. There are three classical answers to that question, and they almost never agree. The mean is the arithmetic average: add all the values and divide by the count. The median is the middle value of the sorted list. The mode is the most frequent value. Each one is the right tool for a different kind of question, and one of the first lessons of statistics is knowing when to reach for which.

Consider the seven values 2, 4, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12. The mean is (2+4+4+6+8+10+12)/7 ≈ 6.57. The median is 6, the fourth value after sorting. The mode is 4, the only repeated value. Three different numbers for the same data; each one a different reasonable answer to “where is the center?” This section defines each measure precisely, computes them on standard examples, and develops the visual intuition that lets us see what each one captures.
5.2.1  Mean: the arithmetic average

The mean of a data set is the sum of the values divided by the number of values. In symbols, if the data set has values x1, x2, ···, xn, the mean is (x1 + x2 + ··· + xn) / n. The bar atop the variable, x̄ (read “x-bar”), is the standard symbol for the sample mean. The mean has a useful physical interpretation: if each data point were a unit weight placed at its value on the number line, the mean is the point at which the line would balance.

content.caption: Figure 5.2. The three measures of center on a single data set. The mode sits at the most-stacked value; the median sits at the middle of the sorted line; the mean sits where the line balances under unit weights.

content.text: Definition 5.2. The mean of a data set with values x1, x2, ···, xn is the value x̄ = (x1 + x2 + ··· + xn) / n. The mean is sensitive to extreme values (outliers).
5.2.2  Median: the middle of the sorted data

The median is the middle value of the data set after it has been sorted. For a data set with an odd number of values n, the median is the single middle value — the value in position (n+1)/2. For an even number of values, there is no single middle, so the median is defined as the average of the two middle values, in positions n/2 and (n/2)+1. Two conventions matter. First, the data must be sorted before the middle is read off; reading the middle of an unsorted list produces nonsense. Second, the median is much less sensitive to outliers than the mean, which is the most common reason to prefer it.

Definition 5.3. The median of a data set is the middle value of the sorted data. For odd n, the median is the value in position (n+1)/2. For even n, the median is the average of the values in positions n/2 and (n/2)+1.

Margin noteEconomic reports almost always quote the median rather than the mean for household income, home prices, and similar quantities, because the underlying distributions are heavily skewed by a small number of very high values. Median income is robust against billionaires; mean income is not.
5.2.3  Mode: the most frequent value

The mode of a data set is the value (or values) that appears most often. Where the mean and median capture the location of the data, the mode captures its peak. A data set with exactly one most-frequent value is unimodal; with two values tied for most frequent it is bimodal; with three or more it is multimodal. If every value appears exactly once (no repeats), the data set has no mode. On the ALEKS interface, the appropriate response in that case is to click the “No mode” button rather than entering a numerical answer.

Three measures of center, three different versions of the question “where is the center?” The mode looks for the peak; the median looks for the middle of the sorted line; the mean looks for the balance point.

Common mistakeReading the median off the unsorted data. The median is the middle of the sorted list, not the middle position of the list as it was originally written. For data set {17, 4, 25, 8, 12}, the entry in position 3 of the original list is 25, but the median — the middle of the sorted list {4, 8, 12, 17, 25} — is 12. Always sort first.

Example 5.2. Three measures on one data set.

Adults being tested for dementia are asked to perform tasks such as listing as many animals as they can in one minute. Here are the numbers of animals listed by 10 adults: 11, 17, 12, 21, 10, 24, 23, 20, 10, 14. Compute the mean, median, and mode.

Solution.

Step 1. Sort. 10, 10, 11, 12, 14, 17, 20, 21, 23, 24. The sorted list serves both the median and the mode in one pass.

Step 2. Median. With n = 10 (even), the median is the average of the values in positions 5 and 6: (14 + 17)/2 = 15.5.

Step 3. Mean. The sum of the ten values is 11+17+12+21+10+24+23+20+10+14 = 162, so x̄ = 162/10 = 16.2.

Step 4. Mode. Looking at the sorted list, only the value 10 repeats (twice); every other value appears once. So there is exactly one mode: 10.

The three measures answer slightly different questions on the same data. The mean 16.2 is the balance point; the median 15.5 is the middle of the sorted line; the mode 10 is the most-stacked value.

In the wildReal-estate listings report the median home price, not the mean, for any given neighborhood. A single $20 million sale at the high end of a market would lift the mean noticeably; the median is determined entirely by the middle of the sorted list of sale prices and is unaffected by such outliers. Mode is less common in financial reporting but appears in product analytics (most-purchased size of a shoe, most-watched length of a podcast episode) where the “most frequent” answer is what matters.
Exercises

1. Find the median of the data set 8, 9, 10, 10, 11, 12, 4, 14, 4, 7, 14, 13 (twelve values).

    Show answer

    Answer. 10. Sorted: 4, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 14. With n = 12 (even), average the values in positions 6 and 7: (10+10)/2 = 10.

  2. Find the mode of 1, 1, 3, 3, 3, 0, 2, 1.

    Show answer

    Answer. 1 and 3. The data set is bimodal: both 1 and 3 appear three times; every other value appears fewer times. List both modes.

  3. Find the mean of 45, 52, 38, 60, 47, 52, 41, 55, 50, 52 (ten values), rounded to one decimal place.

    Show answer

    Answer. 49.2. Sum is 492; mean is 492/10 = 49.2.

  4. For the data set in the previous problem, find the median and mode.

    Show answer

    Answer. Median 51, mode 52. Sorted: 38, 41, 45, 47, 50, 52, 52, 52, 55, 60. Median is (50+52)/2 = 51; 52 is the unique mode (appears three times).

  5. True or false: the mean and median of a data set must always be equal.

    Show answer

    Answer. False. The two are equal when the distribution is symmetric, but in general they differ; the mean is pulled by outliers, while the median is robust to them. The difference between the two is often a useful diagnostic for skewness in the data.

  6. The data set 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 has what mode?

    Show answer

    Answer. No mode. Every value appears exactly once, so no value qualifies as “most frequent.” On the ALEKS interface, click the “No mode” button rather than entering a number.
More worked examples

content.title: All three measures, by hand
content.problem: Find the **mean**, **median**, and **mode** of the data set **{2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 10}**.
content.solution:
**Mean** = sum / count. Sum = 2+3+3+5+7+8+10 = 38. Count = 7. Mean = 38/7 ≈ **5.43**.

**Median.** Sort: 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 10 (already sorted). Seven numbers — the middle one (the 4th) is **5**.

**Mode** = most frequent value. Only **3** appears more than once. **Mode = 3**.

Three different centers, three different stories about the same data: 5.43 is the balance point; 5 is the middle; 3 is the most common.

content.title: Outliers shift the mean, not the median
content.problem: A small data set: **{20, 22, 24, 25, 27, 28, 30}**. Now add an outlier: **{20, 22, 24, 25, 27, 28, 30, 200}**. How does the mean shift, and how does the median shift?
content.solution:
**Original set (no outlier).**

Mean = (20+22+24+25+27+28+30)/7 = 176/7 ≈ **25.14**.

Median = 25 (the 4th of 7).

**With outlier added.**

Mean = (176 + 200)/8 = 376/8 = **47**. (The mean **almost doubled**.)

Median: sort the new set, the two middle values are 25 and 27, so median = 26.

*The takeaway.* The mean is **sensitive** to outliers — one data point at 200 dragged the mean from 25 to 47. The median is **robust** — it only moved from 25 to 26. This is why income, home prices, and other skewed distributions are almost always reported as **medians**, not means.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Three centers, three personalities. **Mean** is the balance point; sensitive to outliers but useful when the data is symmetric. **Median** is the middle value; robust to outliers, ideal for skewed distributions like income. **Mode** is the most common value; useful for categorical data and identifying popularity.

content.text: Looking ahead

The center of a data set is half the descriptive story. The other half is the spread — how widely the values scatter around that center. Section 5.3 introduces the range and the standard deviation, the two standard measures of spread, and shows how center and spread together determine almost everything we will say about the distribution in Sections 5.5 and 5.6.
5.2
